gaps.title: init / switch -c bootstrap leaves the substrate config-less, with no op-driven path to a custom-vocabulary config
gaps.description: initProject (the context-init op) and switchAndCreate (/context switch -c) scaffold the substrate dir + schemas/ subdir but write no config.json. The only op that originates a config is acceptCanonicalConfig/accept-all, which adopts the entire packaged conception (all-or-nothing, never-clobber). amendConfigEntry throws 'no config.json' when the config is absent, so it cannot bootstrap one. Net: a user who wants a substrate with a custom vocabulary rather than the full packaged catalog has no op-driven path and must hand-author config.json, falling off the tool surface.

evidence.reference: initProject scaffolds dirs + pointer only; writes no config.json
switchAndCreate flips pointer + scaffolds dirs only; writes no config.json
amendConfigEntry throws 'no config.json' when config absent — cannot originate a config
adoptConception never-clobber guard if(existing) + whole-catalog adoption — only config-originating op, all-or-nothing
Live dogfooding state: pointer switched to using-pi-context/, which holds only an empty schemas/ subdir and no config.json — the broken state with no ops path forward

gaps.impact: Custom-vocabulary substrate creation is impossible through the tool surface; a dogfooded /context switch -c lands the user on a config-less substrate that no op can advance (accept-all would impose the whole catalog; amend-config refuses without a config).
gaps.proposed_resolution: init + switch -c write a minimal schema-valid skeleton config (schema_version + empty block_kinds + minted substrate_id) via a shared never-clobber writeSkeletonConfig helper; accept-all becomes skeleton-aware (overwrite when absent OR isSkeletonConfig, never-clobber a populated config); deriveBootstrapState gains a 'skeleton' state ordered no-pointer -> no-config -> skeleton -> not-installed -> ready; onward-guidance messages name the two paths (accept-all OR amend/edit).

gaps.title: No substrate clone/import: cross-project substrate reuse has no op-driven path and substrate identity carries no provenance
gaps.description: substrate_id is content-addressed, but the project-root registry (.pi-context-registry.json) is a flat id -> { dir, aliases } map with no origin/provenance. Item-level cross-substrate import exists (promoteItem mints a fresh content-addressed identity + records item_derived_from_item lineage), but there is no substrate-level analogue. Bringing a pi-context substrate in from another project therefore requires manual file copy + a manual registry edit, and yields a false substrate_id_unregistered drift error because the SoT-drift invariant cannot distinguish a native substrate whose registry entry was lost from a legitimately imported foreign one.

evidence.reference: registry entry shape is { dir, aliases } only — no origin/provenance field
SoT-drift invariant: an unregistered config.substrate_id is a single error code with no native-vs-foreign discrimination
promoteItem provides item-level cross-substrate import with item_derived_from_item lineage + fresh content-addressed identity; no substrate-level counterpart exists
the only registerSubstrate call sites record id+dir+aliases, no origin — so an imported substrate's lineage is unrecorded

gaps.impact: No tool-driven cross-project substrate reuse; imported substrates carry no lineage to their origin; the SoT-drift invariant false-positives on legitimate imports; and the local switch-register reconciliation fix cannot tell a native-lost-registry substrate from a foreign import, so registering blindly on switch would silently absorb a foreign or colliding id.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Lift substrate identity to the content-addressed + lineage model items already use: a substrate-level substrate_derived_from_substrate relation, provenance-bearing registry entries (minted-here vs derived-from), and a cloneSubstrate op (fork semantics: copy content into a new dir, mint a fresh substrate_id, record the derived-from edge + origin) surfaced as a dual-surface pi-context clone (library + Pi tool + CLI + orchestrator script). Preserves the one-id<->one-dir SoT invariant. See DEC-0002.

gaps.title: switch / switch -c do not reconcile the active substrate's identity into the registry, yielding a false substrate_id_unregistered
gaps.description: Switching the active pointer to a substrate whose config.json carries a substrate_id absent from this project's .pi-context-registry.json produces a false substrate_id_unregistered error from the SoT-drift invariant, though nothing is functionally broken. Registration happens only in writeSkeletonConfig (fresh write) and adoptConception (accept-all); switchToExisting only checks config presence then flips the pointer, and switchAndCreate's writeSkeletonConfig never-clobber skips the write (and its register) when a config already exists. So neither switch path records the now-active substrate's identity.

evidence.reference: switchToExisting checks config.json exists + flipBootstrapPointer; never calls registerSubstrate
the only registerSubstrate call sites — writeSkeletonConfig fresh write + adoptConception
SoT-drift invariant emits substrate_id_unregistered when config.substrate_id has no registry entry

gaps.impact: A valid switch to an unregistered substrate (copied-in, hand-authored, or surviving a registry reset) fails context-validate with a false error; the remedy today is a manual registerSubstrate or a clobber-guarded accept-all.
gaps.proposed_resolution: switch paths register config.substrate_id -> active dir when the id is absent from the registry (functionally safe: absent => no collision; a present-but-different-dir entry remains the substrate_id_registry_mismatch error). Origin/provenance recording for imported substrates defers to DEC-0002. See DEC-0003.

gaps.title: pi-context has no structure-aware git merge driver — substrate files fall through to git's line-based merge, which mangles them
gaps.description: Substrate state lives in git-tracked files (<block>.json, relations.json, config.json, schemas, objects/). When two branches carrying the same substrate are merged, git's default line-based merge diffs the JSON text, mangling arrays and producing structurally invalid or semantically wrong states. pi-context registers no custom git merge driver, so substrate files fall through to that line merge. The gap: a custom git merge driver (registered via .gitattributes) that, given git's three versions (%O ancestor / %A ours / %B theirs), performs a structure-aware per-file 3-way merge — matching items by stable id/oid rather than array index, merging edges as a set, merging ordered ref-fields via the convergent ordered-sequence field-type (FGAP-005), and merging config/schema — and writes a data-model-valid result; PLUS a post-merge pass for what a per-file driver structurally cannot do: cross-block referential integrity (e.g. an edge whose endpoint item was deleted in another file) and whole-substrate invariant + lens revalidation. Git owns branches, commits, merge-base, refs, and invocation; pi-context owns only the structure-aware reconciliation.

evidence.reference: substrate writes are whole-item upsert; nothing registers a git merge driver, so <block>.json / relations.json merge via git's line-based default, which mangles JSON arrays and yields structurally invalid states
no merge=<driver> attribute declared for substrate files (<block>.json / relations.json / config.json) — they get git's default line merge
content-addressed objects/ are distinct hash-named files (equal hash == equal content), so git's directory merge unions them without conflict — no driver needed there

gaps.impact: Merging two branches that both edited the same substrate corrupts it: concurrent array reorders / inserts produce invalid or wrong JSON, and cross-block referential integrity + declared invariants are never re-checked after a merge. Same-repo collaborative editing on a pi-context substrate is unsafe under normal git workflows.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Ship a git merge driver (.gitattributes merge=pi-context + a [merge "pi-context"] driver entry) performing per-file structure-aware 3-way merge of substrate files from git's %O/%A/%B (items by id/oid, edge-set merge, ordered ref-fields via FGAP-005, config/schema), writing a data-model-valid result; plus a post-merge finalize/validate hook (git post-merge or a merge-finalize command) for cross-block referential integrity + invariant/lens revalidation, emitting a typed conflict set for what cannot auto-resolve. Branches, merge-base, commit lineage, refs, and cherry-pick stay git's. See DEC-0004.

gaps.title: Ordered collections of item-refs need a convergent sequence field-kind — an array index cannot merge concurrent insert / delete / move
gaps.description: Order is a plain ordered array of refs on the owning item (e.g. a fragment_refs / phase-order / step-list field), authoritative by design. Single-writer insert/reorder works: splice the array, one atomic item write. But the array carries no merge semantics: two divergent versions that each reorder it 3-way-merge to a conflict (git) or whole-array last-writer-wins (item upsert), losing one side's ordering. The gap: when two divergent substrate versions each insert, delete, and/or move elements in the same ordered collection, the substrate must merge them into one deterministic, replica-independent sequence containing every insert, with each element retaining a single identity and a single position, and concurrently-inserted runs kept coherent rather than interleaved. An array index cannot carry this, because position is a value two branches generate independently and must reconcile. The capability is latent until the structure-aware git merge driver (FGAP-004) exists, which is the host that invokes this field-level merge on git's three versions of the owning block file.

evidence.reference: ordering today is a plain ordered array of refs on the owning item (authoritative by design); position is an array index two branches generate independently and cannot reconcile
item upsert is whole-array last-writer-wins and git line-merge mangles arrays; neither merges concurrent insert/delete/move on an ordered ref-array — one side's ordering is lost

gaps.impact: Ordered collections of item-refs (roadmaps, milestone sequences, fragment composition, step lists) in substrates branched and merged across operators / sessions / branches cannot converge: concurrent reordering on divergent versions conflicts or silently drops an ordering.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A declared sequence-field kind whose elements carry unique, densely-orderable, immutable position identifiers minted relative to neighbors at insert time (non-interleaving Fugue-class sequence CRDT); move as a first-class op via a position register resolved by hybrid logical clock (Kleppmann move-CRDT) with stable element identity; HLC for replica-independent same-gap ordering + move resolution; a field-aware merge driver composing the per-field CRDT op-sets/states; a sort-on-read projection emitting a plain ordered ref-array (and translating array-position hand-edits back to CRDT ops); authoritative CRDT state in the owning <block>.json, projection + merge driver in block-api. Plugs into the substrate merge driver (FGAP-004) as a field-kind. See DEC-0005.

gaps.title: relations.json has no scoped remove/replace edge op — edge hygiene is append-only through the tool surface
gaps.description: append-relation is a full dual-surface op (library appendRelation + Pi tool + orchestrator script + CLI). There is no counterpart to remove or replace a single edge. Whole-file writeRelations exists (library only, consumed by rename-canonical-id) but there is no scoped remove-relation primitive, no Pi tool, and no CLI op. So a mis-filed edge (wrong direction / type / endpoint — e.g. the TASK-004/TASK-005 dependency just corrected) cannot be fixed through the operator/agent tool surface; only ad-hoc loadRelations+filter+writeRelations library code can, which harness-confined in-pi agents (registered Pi tools only) cannot run.

evidence.reference: append-relation is dual-surface (library appendRelation + Pi tool + CLI + script); no remove/replace counterpart exists anywhere
writeRelations is a whole-file replace (library only, used by rename-canonical-id); grep for removeRelation/deleteRelation/pruneRelation returns none — no scoped single-edge removal
the backwards TASK-005->TASK-004 edge was removable only via orchestrator-side loadRelations+filter+writeRelations (5621c2a), unavailable to cli-only or in-pi-tool-only consumers

gaps.impact: Edges are append-only through tools; mis-directions, typos, and superseded edges accumulate uncorrectably for cli-only and harness-confined consumers, who would need privileged library access to fix them — breaking the dual-surface symmetry append-relation otherwise has.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A scoped remove-relation (and replace-relation) as a dual-surface unit mirroring append-relation: library removeRelation(cwd, {parent, child, relation_type}) (idempotent whole-file rewrite minus the matched edge) + Pi tool + CLI op + orchestrator script; run context-validate after for referential integrity. replace-relation = remove+append in one call for direction/type corrections.

gaps.title: Ordering relation_type names read opposite to the parent/child convention (task_depends_on_task footgun) — backwards edges file silently
gaps.description: relation_type canonical_ids use source_verb_target naming, so task_depends_on_task reads as parent(source) depends_on child(target). But the closure-table convention + the current-state deriver treat parent as the PREREQUISITE and child as the DEPENDENT — the child is blocked until the parent completes (context-sdk.ts:667-722). Name and stored direction are therefore opposite: the name implies the parent is the depender; the system treats the parent as the depended-upon. append-relation does no semantic direction check, so a backwards ordering edge is accepted silently and only surfaces in derived state — it just produced a backwards TASK-004/TASK-005 dependency, corrected at 5621c2a.

evidence.reference: edge-direction contract: {parent: D, child: T} => T blocked until D completed (parent=prerequisite, child=dependent) — opposite to the source_verb_target name reading
relation_type task_depends_on_task display_name 'depends on task', category ordering — name reads 'parent depends on child' while the deriver treats parent as prerequisite
the corrected dependency edge (5621c2a): original parent=TASK-005/child=TASK-004 was filed per the name, wrong per the deriver

gaps.impact: Any ordering relation whose verb implies the parent is the actor while the deriver treats parent as the prerequisite is easy to mis-direct; append-time silence means the error is invisible until topo/blocked derivation. Affects dependency-ordering correctness across tasks and any similarly-shaped ordering relation (e.g. phase_depends_on).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Carry explicit endpoint-role metadata on the relation_type (which endpoint is the prerequisite vs the dependent) and have appendRelation / append-relation validate or normalize an ordering edge's direction against it at write time, so name and stored direction cannot diverge and a backwards edge is rejected (or auto-oriented) at append rather than discovered in derived state. Extends the existing relation-type endpoint-kind metadata; the deriver already consumes the contract, so the fix makes the WRITE path enforce the same contract.

gaps.title: Op-execution contract carries no DispatchContext channel — every cli / in-pi write op is attestation-blind
gaps.description: OpDefinition.run(cwd, params) has no DispatchContext channel (ops-registry.ts:104); registerAll invokes op.run(ctx.cwd, params), threading only ctx.cwd (:1519); the cli injects a writer only when an op's params schema declares a 'writer' property (cli.ts:292). So every write op whose schema omits 'writer' structurally cannot forward the library's ctx?: DispatchContext, and attestation stamping (stampItem, dispatch-context.ts:121-152) never runs. Ten write ops drop the ctx: append/update/remove-block-item, the nested trio, write-block, append-relation, plus amend-config and write-schema (which pass a literal undefined at the ctx slot). On attested-required schemas (framework-gaps / decisions / features require created_by + created_at) the cli filing fails AJV unless the caller hand-authors author fields. The same drop recurs in pi-workflows step-block.ts (six writers). The append-block-item ctx-drop is the seed instance.

evidence.reference: OpDefinition.run(cwd, params) — no DispatchContext channel in the contract (verified)
registerAll invokes op.run(ctx.cwd, params) — pi ExtensionContext identity not threaded (verified)
injectWriter sets params.writer only if the op schema declares a 'writer' property (verified)
append-block-item calls appendToBlock(cwd, block, arrayKey, item) — ctx (4th arg) dropped (verified)
all six block-api writers called without ctx — same drop

gaps.impact: Every cli- or workflow-written item is unattested; the DispatchContext attestation model (created_by stamping; human/agent/monitor/workflow writer kinds) is silently bypassed; attested-required schemas reject cli filings. This is why filing has gone through the orchestrator scripts (which thread ctx), not the cli.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Thread a writer/DispatchContext through the op-execution contract itself (a ctx/writer channel on OpDefinition.run + registerAll + the cli writer-injection) so every write op stamps uniformly — rather than per-op schema smuggling (the channel only promote-item / write-schema-migration / context-switch use today, and which context-archive declares-but-ignores). See DEC-0006.

gaps.title: Op-registry does not cover the library write surface — whole capabilities unreachable from the cli / out-of-pi
gaps.description: Library write capabilities have no op-registry entry, so they are unreachable from pi-context-cli and in-pi tools: edge removal/replace (removeRelation does not exist in the library at all; writeRelations whole-replace has no op), upsertItemInBlock (atomic find-or-replace), writeConfig (whole-config), writeSkeletonConfig, writeSchema/updateSchema (unchecked write + mutator), registerSubstrate/writeRegistry (foreign-substrate registration), writeBootstrapPointer/flipBootstrapPointer (raw pointer), and bulk appendRelations. An operator restricted to the cli cannot correct a mis-filed edge, run an atomic upsert, or write a config/registry/pointer.

evidence.reference: writeRelations (whole-relations replace) + appendRelations (bulk) have no op; removeRelation does not exist — relations are append-only via ops
upsertItemInBlock atomic find-or-replace — no op (used only by pi-behavior-monitors directly)
writeSchema/updateSchema — no op (only write-schema -> writeSchemaChecked)
writeRegistry/registerSubstrate — no op; foreign-substrate registration unreachable from cli
writeBootstrapPointer/flipBootstrapPointer — no op

gaps.impact: The cli is not a faithful interface to the library write surface; whole categories of substrate operation are out-of-pi-unreachable. FGAP-006 (no edge-removal op) is a specific instance, with the sub-layer that the removeRelation library primitive is itself missing.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A systematic op per library write capability as a dual-surface unit (library + Pi tool/op + CLI + orchestrator script): add the missing removeRelation library primitive + a remove-relation / replace-relation op (closing FGAP-006), and ops for writeRelations / upsertItemInBlock / writeConfig / writeSchema / registerSubstrate / bootstrap-pointer writers / bulk appendRelations as the surface warrants. See DEC-0006.

gaps.title: No enforced parity across the library / op-registry / orchestrator-script surfaces — they drift silently
gaps.description: The dual-surface principle ('library + tool + script as a unit') is unenforced. The orchestrator scripts build a DispatchContext and forward ctx + optional params (dryRun / ordinal) that the ops drop; the op-registry omits library write fns entirely; nothing catches the divergence at build/test. So projection loss (FGAP-008) and coverage gaps (FGAP-009) accrue silently — e.g. appendToBlock is called WITH ctx by file-block-item.ts:273 and pi-behavior-monitors:1612 but WITHOUT ctx by op append-block-item (ops-registry.ts:174) and pi-workflows step-block.ts:203.

evidence.reference: builds DispatchContext + passes ctx to appendToBlock — op append-block-item drops it
forwards ordinal + ctx to appendRelationByRef — the op forwards ordinal but drops ctx
no test asserts op-registry coverage of the library write surface or ctx/param forwarding parity

gaps.impact: FGAP-008 and FGAP-009 recur on every new library writer or op without a guard; the three surfaces silently diverge; attestation correctness and cli completeness can regress undetected.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A parity/coverage test (the dual-surface unit made checkable) that fails when (a) a library write fn has no op, or (b) an op drops the ctx / optional params (dryRun, ordinal, ...) its underlying library fn or its orchestrator-script sibling forwards; wired into husky pre-commit + CI. See DEC-0006.

gaps.title: No release/version vocabulary — releases and changelog grouping cannot be planned or tracked as first-class substrate items
gaps.description: The substrate (and the packaged conception) has no release/version block kind, no version field, no target/date, and no ships-in-release relation. A release plan can only be modeled by overloading phase (task_positioned_in_phase) or story (story_contains_task) plus a DEC; nothing represents a release as a first-class object binding a version tag + the set of completed dev-items (closed tasks/gaps) + their changelog lines. Changelog text lives in per-package CHANGELOG.md, enforced only at commit-time by the changelog guard, disconnected from the substrate's dev-item lifecycle. So there is no substrate-native way to plan 'release N ships these items' in advance, nor to verify that a release reflects the organized set of done dev-items.

evidence.reference: block_kinds has no release/version/milestone kind; planning relation_types are phase/story/work-order groupings only (task_positioned_in_phase, story_contains_task), none binding a version or release
the packaged conception ships no release/version block kind — the gap is in the catalog, not just this substrate
version bump + changelog stamping operate on CHANGELOG.md ([Unreleased]) at release time, disconnected from substrate task/gap closure
changelog forward-discipline is a manual practice; no substrate linkage between closed dev-items and changelog entries / releases

gaps.impact: Releases are planned and tracked ad-hoc (overloaded phase/story or a loose DEC); there is no first-class binding of version -> completed dev-items -> changelog lines, so the project risks losing the thread between organized dev work done and what shipped in which release.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A release/version vocabulary: a 'release' block kind (id, version, target/date, status planned|released, summary) + a relation binding closed dev-items to a release (e.g. release_contains_item / task_shipped_in_release / gap_closed_in_release) + optionally a milestone/roadmap instantiation; the release item carries or references the changelog lines for its items, so a release is the organized set of done dev-items with their changelog text — planned in advance and verifiable. Dual-surface (schema + conception registration + ops).
